refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report energy metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the enablement of measurement and visibility—what the system must provide—rather than prescribing that the system 'achieve' a target, which depends on operational decisions, equipment tuning, and worksite conditions outside pure system design. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) remains a validation gate, but the FR itself is now decoupled from outcome ownership and focused on the functional capability required.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system can continuously or periodically capture energy input (e.g., fuel consumed, electrical power drawn) and work output (e.g., tonnage moved, volume excavated, distance traveled under load), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, logs, or telemetry such that a third party can independently verify the calculation against the acceptance criterion over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: Acquire and log energy consumption data (fuel volume, electrical current/voltage, or equivalent) at defined intervals during operation.
Acquire and log work output data (tonnage moved, volume displaced, distance traveled under load, or equivalent task-specific metric) at defined intervals during operation.
Compute energy efficiency metric (energy_per_ton or equivalent ratio) from logged energy and work output data.
Provide visibility of energy efficiency metric to authorized stakeholders (operator, site manager, tele-operator, maintenance) via user interface, reports, or telemetry.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio over evaluation period; compare against target threshold.
DP2: Embed energy consumption tracking within operational control loop; correlate with work output events; compute efficiency metric continuously; flag deviations from target in real-time.
DP3: Establish separate metering subsystem for energy and work output; synchronize timestamps; post-process logs to derive efficiency ratio; validate against acceptance criteria at period end.
DP4: Integrate energy and work output sensors into unified data acquisition layer; stream to centralized analytics engine; compute and report efficiency metrics on-demand and periodically.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Establish separate metering subsystem for energy and work output; synchronize timestamps; post-process logs to derive efficiency ratio; validate against acceptance criteria at period end.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization dependency between energy and work output measurement points—mitigated by explicit synchronization protocol (e.g., common clock reference or event correlation).
Data aggregation logic tightly coupled to specific sensor types—mitigated by defining abstract measurement interfaces (energy in joules/kWh, work in tons) independent of sensor technology.
Reporting format dependency on downstream systems—mitigated by defining canonical data schema (energy_used, ton_moved, period_start, period_end, energy_per_ton) decoupled from presentation layer.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 separates measurement concerns (energy vs. work output) into independent subsystems, reducing coupling. Synchronization is explicit and bounded. Post-processing decouples real-time operation from validation logic, supporting independent evolution of control and reporting.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy and work output can be measured independently without mutual interference. Risk: If measurement instrumentation shares power supply or signal conditioning, cross-talk may corrupt readings. Mitigation: Isolate measurement circuits; validate calibration under operational load.

Assumption: Timestamps can be synchronized to sufficient precision (e.g., ±1 second) across distributed measurement points. Risk: Clock drift or network latency may cause misalignment. Mitigation: Use GPS or NTP time source; log synchronization errors; apply post-hoc correlation if needed.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage) is directly observable or derivable from operational events (e.g., load cell readings, dispatch logs). Risk: Indirect measurement may introduce systematic bias. Mitigation: Validate tonnage measurement against independent reference (e.g., scale house records); document measurement uncertainty.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and establish statistical confidence. Risk: Short periods or high variability may yield unreliable efficiency estimates. Mitigation: Define minimum evaluation duration; establish confidence intervals; flag low-confidence results.

Assumption: Customer-defined energy efficiency target E_target is stable and achievable under stated operational conditions. Risk: Target may be unrealistic or conditions may change mid-evaluation. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility study; establish baseline; define change control process for target revision.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Measurement uncertainty tolerance (e.g., ±5% on energy_per_ton); (2) Minimum evaluation period and statistical confidence level (e.g., 30 days, 95% CI); (3) Operational boundary conditions (e.g., ambient temperature, load profile, duty cycle) under which E_target applies; (4) Data retention and audit trail requirements for acceptance validation. This will constrain DP selection toward appropriate sensor accuracy, synchronization precision, and post-processing rigor.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy and work metering subsystem with timestamp synchronization, post-processing pipeline, and efficiency validation logic

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receive raw energy and work output measurements from Vehicle subsystem with native timestamps

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit synchronized, post-processed efficiency metrics and validation results

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive period-end signals and acceptance criteria thresholds for efficiency validation

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts if efficiency validation fails acceptance criteria

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: metering, synchronization, post-processing, and validation are all energy/efficiency domain concerns. NGHE Solution is the natural owner of energy harvesting and efficiency analysis. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and tasking/safety signaling cross boundaries, which are standard interface contracts.

Poor fit: Vehicle is responsible for actuation and sensing, not energy accounting or efficiency post-processing. Placing metering logic here violates separation of concerns. Would require Vehicle to own domain-specific analysis logic that belongs in NGHE. High diffusion: efficiency validation and post-processing would leak into Vehicle's core responsibilities.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation handles remote command and control, not energy metering or efficiency analysis. No cohesion with DP3. Would introduce severe coupling and architectural confusion. Efficiency validation is orthogonal to teleoperation concerns.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety-critical alerting and validation, but not energy metering or post-processing. DP3 includes domain-specific efficiency analysis that is not a safety concern per se. Placing metering here conflates energy accounting with safety logic, reducing cohesion and increasing coupling to non-safety modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP3 is fundamentally an energy harvesting and efficiency analysis concern. The NGHE Solution is the natural owner because it already encapsulates energy domain logic, measurement aggregation, and performance validation. Metering subsystem, timestamp synchronization, post-processing, and efficiency ratio derivation are all cohesive within the energy harvesting domain. External interfaces (Telemetry for data ingestion, Tasking for period signals, SafetyAlert for validation failures) are clean and minimal, indicating low diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle subsystem owns raw sensor hardware, latency or jitter in timestamp delivery from Vehicle to NGHE Solution could degrade synchronization accuracy. Mitigation: Define strict timestamp contract in Telemetry interface (e.g., hardware-timestamped or synchronized via NTP).

Risk: If acceptance criteria are dynamic or mission-dependent, Tasking interface may become a bottleneck or source of coupling. Mitigation: Parameterize acceptance criteria as configuration data passed via Tasking at period start, not as imperative commands.

Risk: Safety Services may expect to own all validation logic. Mitigation: Clarify that efficiency validation is domain-specific (energy), not safety-critical; Safety Services owns the alert emission pathway, not the efficiency computation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by establishing a dedicated metering subsystem with timestamp synchronization and post-processing to derive the efficiency ratio (energy_per_ton). The DP explicitly includes validation against acceptance criteria at period end, which maps cleanly to the constraint that measured energy_per_ton ≤ E_target. The logical solution (separate metering, synchronization, post-processing, validation) is sound and complete.

diffusion_risks: Timestamp synchronization latency: If Vehicle subsystem owns raw sensor hardware and timestamps are not hardware-synchronized or NTP-aligned before transmission to NGHE Solution via Telemetry interface, jitter could degrade efficiency ratio accuracy. Mitigation: Enforce strict timestamp contract (e.g., hardware-timestamped, synchronized clock source, or bounded latency SLA) in Telemetry interface specification.

Dynamic acceptance criteria coupling: If acceptance criteria thresholds are mission-dependent and passed imperatively via Tasking interface at runtime, this creates tight coupling between Tasking and NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Parameterize acceptance criteria as immutable configuration data at period start; avoid imperative re-tasking mid-period.

Safety Services boundary confusion: If Safety Services attempts to own efficiency validation logic (not just alert emission), this will fragment DP3 across two modules. Mitigation: Clarify architectural contract: NGHE Solution owns efficiency computation and validation logic; Safety Services owns only the alert emission pathway and safety-critical signaling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. All core DP3 responsibilities (metering, synchronization, post-processing, efficiency ratio derivation, validation logic) are cohesive within the energy harvesting and efficiency analysis domain. External interfaces are minimal and well-defined: Telemetry (data ingestion), Tasking (period signals and criteria), SafetyAlert (alert emission). No scattered responsibilities detected. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are poor fits (0.45, 0.2, 0.35 containment respectively) due to lack of domain cohesion and high diffusion risk. NGHE Solution is the correct owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly computable from synchronized energy and work output logs within NGHE Solution. Post-processing pipeline can aggregate measurements over the agreed evaluation period and emit validation results. Verification is deterministic and auditable via log inspection.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution owns the complete DP3 realization: metering subsystem (hardware/firmware), timestamp synchronization logic, post-processing pipeline (software), efficiency ratio computation, and validation against E_target. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous. Interfaces to Vehicle (Telemetry in), Tasking (period signals and criteria in), and Safety Services (SafetyAlert out) are explicit and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Specify timestamp synchronization contract in Telemetry interface: define whether timestamps are hardware-synchronized, NTP-aligned, or subject to bounded latency SLA. Document acceptable jitter tolerance for efficiency ratio accuracy.

Parameterize acceptance criteria (E_target) as immutable configuration passed via Tasking at period start, not as dynamic imperative commands. This reduces coupling and ensures validation logic remains deterministic.

Document architectural contract with Safety Services: NGHE Solution owns efficiency validation computation; Safety Services owns alert emission pathway and safety-critical signaling. Prevent logic fragmentation.
Define period-end signal semantics in Tasking interface: specify whether period-end is a hard deadline, whether in-flight measurements are included, and how partial periods are handled.

Add traceability requirement: NGHE Solution must log all raw measurements, synchronized timestamps, post-processed values, and validation decisions for audit and customer review against acceptance criteria.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR3 and DP3 are logically sound and architecturally well-contained within NGHE Solution. However, the high safety criticality of the efficiency validation function and the identified diffusion risks (timestamp synchronization latency, dynamic acceptance criteria coupling, Safety Services boundary confusion) warrant decomposition of DP3 into sub-level FRs to ensure safety-critical concerns are explicitly addressed, interfaces are hardened, and validation logic is isolated from external imperative coupling. Decomposition will expose: (1) timestamp synchronization as a distinct safety-critical sub-function, (2) acceptance criteria parameterization and immutability as a separate control flow, (3) validation logic isolation from Safety Services, and (4) audit/traceability as an explicit safety-critical requirement. This decomposition is necessary to manage safety risk and prevent architectural diffusion at realization time.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Synchronize energy and work output measurements to a common, bounded-jitter timestamp reference such that timestamp error does not degrade efficiency ratio accuracy below acceptance tolerance.

FR3.2: Ingest and lock acceptance criteria (E_target and evaluation period boundaries) as immutable configuration at period start, preventing mid-period imperative re-tasking that could invalidate validation logic.
FR3.3: Compute efficiency ratio (energy_per_ton) from synchronized, post-processed measurement logs and validate against locked acceptance criteria at period end.

FR3.4: Emit efficiency validation result (pass/fail) and supporting audit log (raw measurements, timestamps, post-processed values, validation decision) to Safety Services and external audit systems for deterministic verification and customer review.

FR3.5: Isolate efficiency validation computation logic within NGHE Solution boundary, preventing Safety Services from owning or fragmenting validation logic; Safety Services owns only alert emission pathway and safety-critical signaling.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound but must be refined to explicitly address the five sub-level concerns identified above. Revise DP3 to include: (1) explicit timestamp synchronization contract (hardware-synchronized or NTP-aligned with bounded latency SLA), (2) immutable configuration pattern for acceptance criteria (passed at period start, locked for duration), (3) deterministic validation logic isolated within NGHE Solution with clear interface to Safety Services (validation result + audit log out, not imperative commands in), (4) comprehensive audit logging and traceability, and (5) architectural contract document clarifying ownership boundaries with Safety Services. These refinements will harden DP3 against the identified diffusion risks and ensure safety-critical validation is not compromised by external coupling or logic fragmentation.

assumptions: Timestamp synchronization can be achieved via hardware clock synchronization or NTP with bounded latency SLA acceptable to efficiency ratio accuracy requirements.
Acceptance criteria (E_target) are mission-dependent but can be parameterized as immutable configuration at period start without requiring mid-period re-tasking.
Safety Services will accept a clear architectural contract: NGHE Solution owns validation logic; Safety Services owns alert emission and safety-critical signaling only.
Audit logging and traceability are feasible within NGHE Solution without significant performance or storage overhead.
Period-end signal semantics (hard deadline, in-flight measurement inclusion, partial period handling) can be defined and enforced via Tasking interface contract.
open_risks: Timestamp synchronization latency: If Vehicle subsystem hardware timestamps are not synchronized before transmission to NGHE Solution, jitter could degrade efficiency ratio accuracy. Mitigation: Enforce strict timestamp contract in Telemetry interface (hardware-synchronized, NTP-aligned, or bounded latency SLA).

Dynamic acceptance criteria coupling: If Tasking interface allows imperative re-tasking of acceptance criteria mid-period, validation logic becomes non-deterministic and safety-critical validation is compromised. Mitigation: Parameterize acceptance criteria as immutable configuration at period start; block mid-period re-tasking.

Safety Services boundary confusion: If Safety Services attempts to own efficiency validation logic (not just alert emission), DP3 will fragment across two modules, creating diffusion and ownership ambiguity. Mitigation: Document explicit architectural contract; NGHE Solution owns validation logic; Safety Services owns alert emission only.

Audit log completeness and integrity: If audit logs are incomplete or tampered with, customer review and deterministic verification are compromised. Mitigation: Enforce immutable audit log storage and cryptographic integrity checks.

Period-end signal semantics: If period-end signal is ambiguous (hard deadline vs. soft signal, in-flight measurement inclusion unclear), validation results may be inconsistent or disputed. Mitigation: Define and enforce clear period-end semantics in Tasking interface contract.
evidence_needed: Timestamp synchronization specification: Define whether timestamps are hardware-synchronized, NTP-aligned, or subject to bounded latency SLA. Quantify acceptable jitter tolerance for efficiency ratio accuracy.

Acceptance criteria parameterization design: Document immutable configuration pattern for E_target and period boundaries. Specify how criteria are passed via Tasking at period start and locked for duration.

Validation logic isolation design: Document architectural contract with Safety Services. Specify NGHE Solution ownership of validation computation; Safety Services ownership of alert emission only. Provide interface specification (validation result + audit log out).
Audit logging specification: Define audit log schema (raw measurements, synchronized timestamps, post-processed values, validation decision). Specify storage, integrity, and retention requirements.

Period-end signal semantics: Define period-end signal contract in Tasking interface. Specify whether period-end is hard deadline, how in-flight measurements are handled, and how partial periods are resolved.

Safety-critical validation test plan: Define test cases for timestamp synchronization accuracy, acceptance criteria immutability, validation logic correctness, and audit log completeness. Include failure mode analysis for each sub-function.
